title: Flipkart Warehouse Unique Paths
description: A Flipkart picker bot starts at the top-left of an `m x n` warehouse aisle grid and must reach the bottom-right packing station. From any cell it can only move one step right or one step down. Count the number of distinct paths the bot can take.

examples.explanation: DR DR D, DDR DR, etc. — 3 distinct paths.

hints: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]; first row and column = 1.
1D array se space O(n) ho jayega.
Closed-form bhi hai: C(m+n-2, m-1) — but DP simpler hai.

title: Razorpay Payment State Grid With Obstacles
description: Razorpay models a payment state machine as an `m x n` grid: starting at INIT (top-left), the payment must reach SETTLED (bottom-right). From any state you may transition right or down only. Some cells are blocked (regulatory rejects, marked as 1). Given grid `obstacles`, count the number of valid transition paths from start to end. If the start or end cell itself is blocked, the answer is 0.
hints: Same recurrence as unique paths, but blocked cells force dp[i][j] = 0.
Start/end blocked? — return 0 fast.
1D rolling array trick still works.

title: Mumbai-Delhi Grid Minimum Toll
description: A trucker drives through a highway grid from Mumbai (top-left) to Delhi (bottom-right). Each cell has a toll cost (non-negative). The truck can only move right or down at every junction. Return the minimum total toll paid along any path from start to end.
hints: dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
First row and first column: cumulative sum.
1D rolling array — overwrite row in place.

title: Cricket Scoreboard Longest Common Subsequence
description: Two cricket innings are summarized as ball-by-ball strings (e.g., '146024' = runs per ball). A commentator wants the longest common subsequence (LCS) of two such strings — the longest scoring pattern shared in order, but not necessarily contiguous. Return the length of the LCS of `a` and `b`.
hints: Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).

title: Infosys Longest Common Substring
description: Infosys's plagiarism tool checks two code submissions and reports the length of the longest common contiguous substring. Given strings `s` and `t`, return the length of the longest substring that appears in both.
hints: Different from LCS — substring must be contiguous.
dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 if s[i-1]==t[j-1] else 0.
Track global max as you fill the table.

title: Swiggy Address Edit Distance
description: Swiggy's address dedupe pipeline measures the minimum number of single-character edits (insert, delete, replace) to convert string `s` into string `t` (Levenshtein distance). Return that minimum number.
hints: dp[i][j] = edits to transform s[0..i-1] into t[0..j-1].
If chars match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1].
Else: 1 + min(dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).

title: Ola Driver Name One Edit Distance
description: Ola's driver onboarding flags identity records that look like typos: two strings `s` and `t` are 'one edit distance' apart iff they differ by exactly one insert, one delete, or one replace. Return `true` only if the edit distance is exactly 1 (not 0, not >1).
hints: Length diff > 1 ⇒ false.
Same length: count mismatched chars; exactly 1 ⇒ true.
Length diff = 1: try skipping the extra char in the longer one — rest must match.

title: Amazon Distinct Subsequence Count
description: Given strings `s` and `t`, count the number of distinct subsequences of `s` that exactly equal `t`. A subsequence preserves relative order but can skip characters. Return the count.
constraints: Answer fits in 32-bit signed int per test data.
hints: dp[i][j] = ways to form t[0..j-1] from s[0..i-1].
Else: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j].

title: Google Interleaving Log Streams
description: Two services emit log strings `a` and `b`. The aggregator merges them while preserving each stream's internal order. Given `a`, `b`, and a candidate merged string `c`, return `true` if `c` is a valid interleaving of `a` and `b` (using all chars from each).
hints: If a.length + b.length != c.length, false.
dp[i][j] = can c[0..i+j-1] be formed from a[0..i-1] + b[0..j-1].

title: Meta Regex Match With Dot And Star
description: Implement regular-expression matching with `.` (matches any single character) and `*` (matches zero or more of the preceding element). The match must cover the entire input string `s`, not just part of it. Given `s` and pattern `p`, return whether `p` matches `s` exactly.
constraints: It is guaranteed each '*' has a valid preceding element.
hints: dp[i][j] = does p[0..j-1] match s[0..i-1]?
If p[j-1] is '*': either zero of preceding (dp[i][j-2]) or one more if s[i-1] matches p[j-2] (dp[i-1][j]).

title: LinkedIn Wildcard Profile Matching
description: LinkedIn's recruiter search supports wildcards in the title pattern: `?` matches any single character, `*` matches any sequence (possibly empty). Given a candidate title `s` and pattern `p`, return whether `p` fully matches `s`.
hints: dp[i][j] = does p[0..j-1] match s[0..i-1]?

title: Myntra Count Palindromic Brand Substrings
description: Myntra's brand-name analyzer wants to count palindromic substrings inside a brand string `s`. Two substrings are different if they start or end at different indices. Return the total count of palindromic substrings in `s`.
hints: dp[i][j] = is s[i..j] a palindrome?
Or use expand-around-center for O(n^2) time, O(1) space.

title: Nykaa Longest Palindromic Substring
description: Find the longest palindromic substring of a string `s`. If multiple substrings tie for the maximum length, return the one with the smaller starting index.

examples.explanation: 'aba' is also valid; we return the earliest.

constraints: s contains lowercase and uppercase English letters.
hints: dp[i][j] = is s[i..j] palindrome — fill by increasing length.
Track best window; on tie keep the earlier-starting one.
Expand-around-center (O(n^2) time, O(1) space) is the common alternative.

title: Google Longest Palindromic Subsequence
description: Given a string `s`, return the length of the longest palindromic subsequence — characters in the same relative order as in `s`, not necessarily contiguous, that read the same forwards and backwards.

examples.explanation: 'bbbb' is a palindromic subsequence of length 4.

hints: dp[i][j] = LPS length of s[i..j].
Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Equivalent to LCS(s, reverse(s)).

title: TCS Matrix Chain Multiplication Cost
description: Given dimensions of `n` matrices in array `dims` of length `n+1` (matrix `i` has dimensions `dims[i] x dims[i+1]`), determine the minimum number of scalar multiplications required to compute the product `M1 * M2 * ... * Mn` by parenthesizing optimally.
hints: dp[i][j] = min cost to multiply matrices i..j.
Try every split point k: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + dims[i]*dims[k+1]*dims[j+1]).
Fill by increasing chain length.

title: Paytm Coin Change Count Of Ways
description: Paytm offers an unlimited supply of denominations in `coins`. Given a target `amount`, count the number of distinct ways to make it. Order does not matter (e.g., 1+2 and 2+1 count once).
hints: dp[i][a] = ways using first i coins to make amount a.
dp[i][a] = dp[i-1][a] + dp[i][a - coin] (skip or use current).
1D rolling: outer over coins, inner over amount ascending — that order avoids ordering double-counts.

title: Amazon Warehouse 0/1 Knapsack
description: An Amazon picker has a knapsack with maximum capacity `W`. Each item `i` has weight `weights[i]` and value `values[i]`. Each item can be taken at most once. Return the maximum total value achievable without exceeding capacity.
hints: dp[i][w] = best value using first i items with capacity w.
Skip: dp[i-1][w]. Take (if fits): dp[i-1][w-weights[i-1]] + values[i-1].
Roll to 1D — iterate w from W down to weights[i-1] (descending) so each item is used once.

title: Olympics Stone Game First Player Wins
description: Two players take turns picking from either end of a row of stones with non-negative integer values `piles`. Player 1 goes first. Both play optimally to maximize their own total. Return `true` if Player 1 ends with strictly more total than Player 2; otherwise `false` (ties count as not winning).

examples.explanation: Whichever end P1 picks, P2 can grab the 5.

hints: dp[i][j] = max score difference (current player - opponent) achievable on piles[i..j].
dp[i][j] = max(piles[i] - dp[i+1][j], piles[j] - dp[i][j-1]).
Answer: dp[0][n-1] > 0.
